delivery_rules: First line: clear, confident broadcast authority
Final song intro slightly warmer than rest

Soften tone slightly before first-song intro
pause_guidance: 0.5–1 second breath before first-song intro line
intros.script_body:
Hey there — you’re tuned to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

Now, before we get into anything tonight, I should probably admit there was a very specific era — let’s call it 2004 — where I believed my AIM away message was doing important emotional work.

Which mostly meant posting, “With my lightning bolts a-glowin’, I can see where I am goin’,” like I had personally joined Arcade Fire.

I had not.

I was just in my bedroom feeling aggressively profound.

I also maintained a three-inch binder of burned CDs. Handwritten track lists. Sequenced like I was curating a museum exhibit called Teen Angst, But Make It Indie.

The first song had to say something. About me. About destiny. About vibes.

I was fourteen.

But here’s what I was accidentally right about — the first track sets the tone.

Not in a dramatic, life-altering way. Just in a steady, confident way.

And I’ve noticed you’ve got a few albums you refuse to retire. The kind that survive every new phase.

Which tells me you respect a strong opener.

So let’s do this properly.

Starting us off with "[SONG TITLE]" by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Ease into story naturally — not abrupt
Let sarcasm land without pushing it
Final song intro grounded and smooth
emphasis_notes: Deadpan emphasis on “nobody asked me to”
pause_guidance: Micro pause after “nobody asked me to”
intros.script_body:
Hey how's it going — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

Now, that calm, confident radio voice you just heard? That took years.

Because there was a stretch in college where I voluntarily signed up for the six a.m. Sunday slot.

Nobody asked me to.

Nobody was awake.

I just believed — very sincerely — that someone out there needed to hear The White Stripes before breakfast.

While everyone else was at actual parties, I was in a studio with burnt coffee, fluorescent lighting, and a playlist that required emotional stamina.

And honestly? I stand by it.

The first song matters most when there’s no one to impress. When it’s just you and the quiet.

I’ve noticed you’ve got a few records you return to when things slow down. The kind that don’t demand attention — they reward it.

That’s my favorite kind of listener.

So let’s begin with something intentional.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

Station ID delivered smoothly and intentionally
First-song intro slightly warmer than rest

intros.script_body:
Hey, glad you're here, I’m Casey.

There was a band I saw once in a room with maybe forty people — sticky floors, questionable acoustics, deeply committed audience.

Two years later they were headlining festivals.

I took that personally.

I’ve always had a soft spot for records that feel like secrets before they feel like headlines. The kind you discover early and then guard like you contributed somehow.

I’ve noticed you’ve got a few albums like that too. The ones you’ve been holding onto long after they stopped being trendy.

That’s a good instinct.

So let’s start with something that feels earned, not obvious.

You’re listening to W.A.I.V.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Do not oversell the breakup angle
emphasis_notes: Deadpan emphasis on “for about four straight months”
intros.script_body:
Hey there — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

There was a breakup in college that I processed very responsibly.

By which I mean I replayed the same album for about four straight months and told people I was “analyzing the production.”

I was not analyzing the production.

I was just dramatically reinterpreting lyrics that had nothing to do with me and acting like they did.

But here’s what that phase did teach me — the right record can carry you through something quietly. No spectacle. No theatrics. Just steady.

I’ve noticed you’ve got a few albums you’ve leaned on like that too. The ones that show up again and again when the mood shifts.

That kind of loyalty says something.

So let’s begin with something that holds its weight.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

emphasis_notes: Deadpan emphasis on “we had to commit”
intros.script_body:
Hey what's up — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

I’m what I like to call a geriatric millennial, which means I remember when discovering music required effort.

You couldn’t just tap and scroll. You had to commit.

You downloaded something questionable from LimeWire, waited twenty minutes, and hoped it wasn’t mislabeled or legally complicated.

It built character.

But it also built taste.

When you had to work for a record, you lived with it longer. You didn’t skip in the first ten seconds. You let it unfold.

And I’ve noticed you’ve got that same patience. There are albums in your rotation that you don’t treat like background noise.

That’s rare.

So let’s start with something that rewards attention.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: No warmth swell until final line
emphasis_notes: Deadpan emphasis on “I absolutely did that”
pause_guidance: Micro pause after “I absolutely did that”
intros.script_body:
Hey what's up — you’re tuned to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

You know, there was a phase where I discovered music almost exclusively through early-2000s review sites.

Long essays. Strong opinions. Decimal points that felt like verdicts.

And yes — I would read the review before deciding if I was emotionally prepared for the album.

I absolutely did that.

It sounds dramatic now, but when you invest that much thought before even pressing play, you tend to listen differently. You don’t skim. You don’t half-commit. You give the record room to prove itself.

I’ve seen that same kind of deliberateness in what you’ve been playing lately. There’s intention there.

So we’re starting with something that doesn’t rush to win you over.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

intros.script_body:
Hey what's up, glad you're here today. So earlier tonight, John was explaining pressing quality to Marcus like it was a sacred philosophy.

He had diagrams.

I respect the commitment.

I tend to be less concerned with how heavy the vinyl feels and more concerned with whether the first thirty seconds make me stay in the room.

That’s always been my test. If a record can hold your attention before it does anything impressive, it’s probably worth your time.

I’ve noticed you don’t skip quickly either. There are a few albums in your rotation that you let unfold instead of fast-forwarding to the hook.

That tells me you listen the way I do.

You’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

So let’s begin with something that earns that kind of patience.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Deliver Marcus reference with affection, not critique
emphasis_notes: Slight amused tone on “he’s not wrong”
intros.script_body:
Hey — Welcome to W.A.I.V. Thanks for being here today. I’m Casey.

Gotta tell you this before we start: Marcus has this habit of describing songs as “textured.”

He says it like he’s carefully evaluating fabric.

And honestly — he’s not wrong.

There’s something about records that reveal themselves slowly. The ones that don’t rush the hook. The ones that let you sit in the first thirty seconds without trying to impress you.

I’ve noticed you lean toward that kind of pacing too. You don’t skip at the first sign of subtlety.

That tells me you’re listening for something more than just volume.

So let’s begin with something that unfolds instead of announces itself.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Deliver Luna reference gently, not jokey
Let the “we wouldn’t have been friends” line land casually
emphasis_notes: Light emphasis on “we would not have been friends”
Slight warmth on “and now she’s one of my favorite people”
pause_guidance: Micro pause after “we would not have been friends”
intros.script_body:
Hey — thanks for joing me today on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

So a quick story before we start: Luna and I were talking the other night about the albums that shaped us in high school.

And we realized something.

If we’d met back then, we would not have been friends.

I was deep in distorted guitars and blog-era drama. She was dissecting chord structures and listening to records that required emotional maturity.

We would have absolutely judged each other.

And now she’s one of my favorite people to argue about music with.

It’s funny how taste evolves. Or maybe it just widens.

I’ve noticed your library does that too — it stretches. There’s contrast in there. Different moods coexisting without apology.

That’s always more interesting than picking one lane.

So let’s start with something that lives comfortably between worlds.

Starting us off with “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Deliver John reference with understated amusement
No recycled phrasing from previous intros
Let humor sit in the dryness
intros.script_body:
Hello there — you’re tuned to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

Before we get into it, did you know that John once spent twenty minutes explaining how pressing quality alters the emotional architecture of an album.

He brought charts.

Actual charts.

Meanwhile, I just want to know if the first thirty seconds make me stop pacing.

We approach things differently.

But we both care about the kind of records that reveal something new on the fourth listen — not just the first.

Looking at your rotation, I can tell you don’t abandon albums after one pass either. There’s patience there. You revisit things.

That’s usually where the good stuff lives.

So we’re starting with something built for a second listen.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Deliver “I used to judge it” casually, not apologetically
Let the shift in perspective feel real but restrained
emphasis_notes: Light emphasis on “I used to dismiss it”
intros.script_body:
Hey what's up — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

I used to dismiss country music.

Not loudly. Just internally.

I filed it away as predictable. Too tidy. Too on-the-nose.

Then I met Jolene.

And it turns out, when someone who actually understands a genre walks you through it, you start hearing the craft. The storytelling. The restraint. The lines that land harder because they aren’t trying to be clever.

I had to revise my opinion.

Looking at your library, I can see that kind of openness in there too. You don’t stick to one lane. There’s indie next to folk next to something that probably surprised you the first time you liked it.

That’s how your taste grows.

So let’s start with something that proves good writing doesn’t need distortion pedals.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Mention The Strokes with subtle reverence, not fan-girl energy
Let Jolene influence feel like evolution, not redemption
intros.script_body:
Hey — appreciate you tuning in today to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

You know, the Strokes were the band that unlocked everything for me.

I remember hearing “The room is on fire and she’s fixing her hair” and realizing music could feel careless and precise at the same time.

They were huge, obviously. But they made me curious about the bands orbiting them. The smaller names in the liner notes. The openers on the tour poster. That curiosity turned into a habit.

I started chasing the edges.

Jolene likes to remind me that curiosity works both ways. She pulled me toward country the same way The Strokes once pulled me toward indie — not by arguing, just by playing me something undeniable.

Looking at your library, I see that same instinct. You don’t stop at the obvious track. You dig a little deeper. There’s range in there.

That’s where the good stuff hides.

So let’s open with something that rewards that kind of curiosity.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Mention The Strokes as gateway, not obsession
Let Magnolia Electric Co. reference feel natural, not performative
Avoid sounding like you’re testing the listener
Tie curiosity to their listening patterns gently
emphasis_notes: Slight softness when referencing deep cut discovery
intros.script_body:
Hello there — thanks for tunning in to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

You know, I’ve mentioned The Strokes were the band that cracked the door open for me.

They made messy feel intentional. They made cool feel attainable.

But what really changed me was what came after.

One late night, chasing recommendations through a blog comment section, I landed on a Magnolia Electric Co. record — the kind of album you don’t find unless you’re already looking for something quieter and heavier at the same time.

That was the moment I realized I didn’t just like music. I liked the hunt.

And looking at your library, I can tell you do too. There are a few artists in there that don’t surface unless you’re willing to dig past the headline names.

That kind of listening changes you a little.

So let’s start with something that rewards curiosity.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Roast the listener gently, not harshly
Include self-comparison so it doesn’t feel one-sided
intros.script_body:
Hey — thanks for tuning in to W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

I noticed something in your listening history.

When you like a band, you really like them.

There are at least three artists in your rotation where you’ve played the same two songs… repeatedly.

That’s commitment.

I say that without judgment. I once listened to a single Bright Eyes track for an entire week and told myself I was “processing.”

But it tells me you find comfort in certain songs. You return to them. You don’t rotate just for the sake of novelty.

So I built this session with that in mind — something familiar at the center, but with a few edges that might stretch you just enough.

Let’s start with one you can settle into.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].

delivery_rules: Include a subtle audible pause (1–1.5 seconds)
emphasis_notes: Slight amused tone when referencing John
intros.script_body:
Hey thanks for being here — you’re on W.A.I.V. I’m Casey.

You know, the first time I realized a song could completely reroute your mood — like physically shift the air in a room — and—

[brief pause, faint off-mic distraction]

Okay. So John just came in very excited about a first press he found this weekend.

Apparently the matrix numbers are “immaculate.”

He says you can hear the difference in the space between the instruments.

I didn’t argue.

You know, it’s funny how some listeners chase fidelity, and some chase feeling.

Looking at your rotation, I can see you lean toward the feeling. You replay songs that hit emotionally, not just technically.

That’s always the better compass.

So let’s start with something that moves the air a little.

Here’s “[SONG TITLE]” by [ARTIST].
